title: Field Briefing: Native Media and Equivalent Access
summary: Field Briefing: Native Media and Equivalent Access uses an original media access briefing scenario to retrieve earlier HTML skills, add a bounded artifact layer, and verify transfer with source and browser evidence.
objectives: Retrieve html audio, html video without step-by-step imitation.
Produce and verify a real media access briefing artifact or evidence report.
Preserve earlier valid requirements while diagnosing changed content and constraints.

steps.instruction: Checkpoint 1: Which claim should the team test first in the media access briefing? Work context: media access briefing.
steps.why: Field Briefing: Native Media and Equivalent Access requires this decision because each decision must preserve document meaning, user control, portability, and inspectable evidence. This is checkpoint 1 in the media access briefing.

options.text: Media access briefing case 1: autoplay is required so keyboard users can discover that audio exists.
Media access briefing case 1: usable audio needs controls, supported sources, fallback, and an equivalent transcript.
Media access briefing case 1: a one-sentence summary is always equivalent to a detailed emergency advisory.

media access briefing phase 2.2: create an audio element with native controls
media access briefing phase 2.3: offer suitable sources and fallback content
media access briefing phase 2.4: link the player to the full transcript and test keyboard operation

steps.title: Read the media access briefing contract · theory
steps.instruction: Checkpoint 4: Which practice follows the explanation and remains valid in a changed document? Work context: media access briefing.
steps.why: Field Briefing: Native Media and Equivalent Access requires this decision because each decision must preserve document meaning, user control, portability, and inspectable evidence. This is checkpoint 4 in the media access briefing.

content.text: media access briefing: Native audio controls provide established playback behavior. Multiple source elements can offer compatible formats, fallback content supports browsers that cannot play them, and a full transcript gives equivalent access to the spoken information without requiring sound. A usable video exposes controls, dimensions, compatible sources, synchronized captions, and fallback content. Captions communicate dialogue and relevant sound; a transcript supports reading and search; and avoiding forced autoplay preserves user control.

hints: Field Briefing: Native Media and Equivalent Access: first isolate the evidence required by “Read the media access briefing contract.”
Field Briefing: Native Media and Equivalent Access: compare the current artifact with the named read goal for step 4.
Field Briefing: Native Media and Equivalent Access: use the smallest defensible change or claim that satisfies “Read the media access briefing contract.”

Media access briefing case 4: usable audio needs controls, supported sources, fallback, and an equivalent transcript.
Media access briefing case 4: once an audio file plays, controls, fallback, and a transcript are optional accessibility extras.
